title: Structure-Preserving Quantum Method of Lines for Evolutionary PDEs with Mixed Boundary Conditions
abstract: We give detailed analysis and circuit design of structure-preserving quantum algorithms for second-order linear evolutionary PDEs, including parabolic equations and hyperbolic equations with mixed Dirichlet, Neumann, and periodic boundary conditions and source terms. While prior quantum algorithms usually neglect the stability problem from the PDE-to-ODE reduction, our method-of-lines approach investigates the boundary lifting via Coons interpolation and boundary-aware discretization, so that the resulting semi-discrete systems are stable and compatible with efficient quantum ODE primitives. For the parabolic problem, we use a diagonal similarity transform to ensure the semi-discrete generator must have a positive semi-definite Hermitian part, and then solve the resulting ODE system by the optimal linear combination of Hamiltonian simulation (LCHS). For the hyperbolic problem, we rewrite the semi-discrete equation as an equivalent first-order system and solve it by Hamiltonian simulation. We implement our quantum algorithms with explicit block-encoding constructions and circuit implementations, as well as demonstrating the end-to-end complexity bounds together with spatial and quadrature error estimates. We conduct classical numerical experiments on the convection-diffusion equation, inhomogeneous heat equation, and Klein-Gordon equation to validate our structure-preserving analysis and algorithmic constructions.
summary: This paper presents a comprehensive, structure-preserving quantum method of lines for solving linear PDEs, covering both parabolic and hyperbolic types. It rigorously derives complexity bounds for the quantum simulation, ensuring that the numerical discretization steps maintain the mathematical structure required by quantum algorithms like LCHS and Hamiltonian simulation. This offers a powerful theoretical tool for quantum simulation in physics.
main_problem: Developing structure-preserving quantum algorithms to solve second-order linear evolutionary Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) with mixed boundary conditions.
main_result: The authors provide complete complexity bounds for the end-to-end quantum solver framework for both parabolic and hyperbolic PDEs, ensuring structural stability throughout the process.
method: The method uses the Method of Lines (MoL) to reduce PDEs to ODEs, which are then solved quantumly using Linear Combination of Hamiltonian Simulation (LCHS) for parabolic cases and direct Hamiltonian simulation for hyperbolic cases.
model_or_system: The focus is on linear evolutionary PDEs, specifically parabolic (e.g., convection-diffusion, heat equation) and hyperbolic (e.g., Klein-Gordon) types, defined on a rectangular domain with mixed Dirichlet, Neumann, and periodic boundary conditions.
key_observables: The primary observable is the quantum state $|	ext{ev}angle$ approximating the solution $u(x, T)$ at time $T$, with error bounded by $\epsilon$.
important_parameters: The complexity depends on the final time $T$, spatial dimension $d$, and the desired error tolerance $\epsilon$.
main_assumptions: The method assumes the solution $u$ is sufficiently smooth and relies on specific structural properties (like positive semi-definiteness) of the semi-discrete operator to utilize quantum primitives.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate error convergence (showing second-order accuracy) for parabolic PDEs using similarity transforms and comparing different kernel functions for the LCHS method.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops the framework by first analyzing the parabolic case (using LCHS and similarity transforms) and then the hyperbolic case (using Hamiltonian simulation). It concludes with detailed complexity bounds and numerical validation across various PDE types and boundary conditions.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, resource-bounded quantum algorithm blueprint for solving fundamental PDEs, bridging the gap between continuous physics models and discrete quantum computation primitives.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
